clinical_scenario: A patient with amyloid-positive (PET) mild AD dementia, no anticoagulation, <=4 cerebral microhemorrhages on MRI is APOE APOE e3/e4. Which treatment is most appropriate?
rationale: Confirmed amyloid early symptomatic AD without ARIA contraindications is an appropriate anti-amyloid candidate with scheduled MRI monitoring.

clinical_scenario: A patient with amyloid-positive mild AD dementia on chronic anticoagulation for atrial fibrillation is APOE APOE e4/e4. Which treatment is most appropriate?
rationale: Concurrent anticoagulation markedly raises ARIA-related hemorrhage risk; anti-amyloid mAbs are generally avoided and symptomatic therapy is appropriate.

clinical_scenario: A patient with amyloid-positive MCI due to AD with MRI showing >4 cerebral microhemorrhages is APOE APOE e3/e3. Which treatment is most appropriate?
rationale: More than four microhemorrhages or prior macrohemorrhage are AUC exclusions for anti-amyloid mAbs due to ARIA-H risk.

clinical_scenario: A patient with moderate-to-severe AD dementia (MMSE 12) is APOE APOE e3/e3. Which treatment is most appropriate?
rationale: Anti-amyloid mAbs are indicated only for MCI / mild AD dementia; memantine is appropriate for moderate-to-severe disease.

clinical_scenario: A patient with mild AD dementia with symptomatic bradycardia and sick sinus syndrome is APOE APOE e3/e4. Which treatment is most appropriate?
rationale: Cholinesterase inhibitors increase vagal tone and can worsen bradycardia or cause syncope; memantine avoids cholinergic cardiac effects.
